lens.included_activities: US graphic-design firms in the lower-middle-market band that provide repeat or recurring outsourced visual-communication work to external customers, including brand-system upkeep, campaign production, packaging refreshes, and production design.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off freelance commissions without repeat service, captive in-house creative teams, fine-art sales, display-ad placement, and firms without transferable operating businesses.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External business customers; a mix of project, hourly, fixed-fee, and retainer revenue, with the screened population limited to recurring or repeat outsourced relationships.
lens.deviation_from_default: The NAICS includes independent commercial artists and project-only work alongside agencies; the lens retains only repeat or recurring outsourced-service firms because a control-transfer and retained-operator screen is otherwise incoherent.

a.bridge: S2 measures task content for the Graphic Designers occupation, not the wage-weighted occupation mix of NAICS 541430. I map its computer-graphics, layout, concept, and collaboration tasks to the industry workforce and count only ideation, asset variation, layout, production, and administrative portions as exposed; this likely overstates exposure where art direction, client discovery, and accountable approval occupy more paid time.
a.rationale: O*NET lists developing graphics and layouts, creating concepts and sample layouts, computer-generated graphics, and collaboration. Generative tools can substitute or avoid hiring in first-pass concepts, asset variants, resizing, layout, and production, but not fully in client discovery, brand judgment, approvals, and final accountability.
a.change_evidence: A 541430 payroll-by-occupation extract paired with a time-and-motion survey of agency teams, segmented by retainer versus project work, could replace the occupation proxy.
caveats: Graphic Designers are employed across many industries, while 541430 also employs account, production, and leadership roles.
The proxy identifies task types but does not measure current AI substitution rates or wage shares.

rho.rationale: More than half of arts and design alumni report using generative AI at work, indicating a workable adoption base. Implementation remains constrained by client approvals, brand consistency, IP review, source-file production, and quality control; therefore only a moderate share of exposed work is expected to become operationally implemented in five years.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US graphic-design agencies showing tool use by workflow, realized cycle-time change, and headcount avoided would materially move the estimate.
caveats: The arts-and-design alumni survey is not a NAICS 541430 agency sample and reports use rather than realized labor removal.
Tool capability and client policy may change faster than the evidence vintage.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition spans branding, printed materials, packaging, advertising, signage, and illustrations. Many providers are one-person or project-oriented; the eligible subset is limited to firms with repeat external service relationships and transferable operations, so eligibility is materially below the whole LMM count.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level database screen of 541430 companies for revenue concentration, contract type, employee count, and owner dependence, validated by broker interviews, could measure this directly.
caveats: Census establishment counts are not LMM firm counts and do not identify recurrence, revenue concentration, or transferability.
The injected LMM count is intentionally not re-estimated here.

s5.rationale: Owner-aging evidence supports a meaningful succession pipeline, but a qualifying control transfer is less common than retirement intent because agencies can wind down, lose key clients, sell only assets, or remain owner-dependent. The range is therefore below the broad share of owners nearing retirement.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year 541430 transaction data from a named lower-middle-market broker or PitchBook/DealStats export, plus state business-registration ownership changes, would materially change this estimate.
caveats: The owner-age statistic is broad small-business evidence rather than a 541430 measure.
It does not distinguish sales from closure, family succession, recapitalization, or internal reorganization.

q.rationale: Repeat design relationships and brand-system knowledge can retain some workflow savings, but project, hourly, and competitive agency pricing expose productivity gains to client renegotiation and lower-cost alternatives. Retainers and fixed-fee scopes support the upper end; commoditized production work supports the lower end.
q.change_evidence: Client contracts and renewal cohorts from 541430 agencies, showing fee basis, rate-card revisions, scope volumes, and gross margin before and after AI workflow deployment, would materially move the estimate.
caveats: No public industry dataset was found that measures AI savings retained by 541430 operators.
Copyright and approval requirements may preserve differentiated human work but do not ensure pricing power.

d5.rationale: BLS projects Graphic Designer employment to grow 2 percent over 2024 to 2034 and cites continuing digital-presence needs, but also explicitly says AI may reduce contracting with freelance graphic designers. For the current outsourced service basket, those opposing forces support a near-flat to modestly declining five-year constant-quality demand estimate.
d5.change_evidence: Annual 541430 receipts deflated for design-service prices, split between recurring agency revenue and project/freelance revenue, plus customer procurement surveys, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Occupation employment projections are not constant-price receipts or NAICS 541430 demand.
The projection horizon is ten years and includes employment in other industries.

o.rationale: An accountable operator is likely to remain needed for brand strategy, stakeholder alignment, production-ready files, review, and rights-sensitive work. Lower-complexity social, template, and first-pass asset work is increasingly self-served or software-supplied, limiting the operator-required share.
o.change_evidence: A buyer survey separating outsourced design spend into self-serve, software-only, and agency-managed workflows, together with agency revenue cohorts by service line, would materially move the estimate.
caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share for this NAICS.
Copyright registration guidance is a constraint on some AI-generated work, not a measure of customer demand for agencies.

narrative.executive_view: Graphic Design Services combines repeatable production work with client-facing creative judgment. The narrowed lens isolates agencies and studios with recurring external relationships, while recognizing that the broader NAICS contains many one-off and owner-dependent providers.
narrative.ai_impact: O*NET task descriptions point to graphics, layouts, concepts, and computer-generated assets as workflow areas where AI can accelerate first passes and production. Human work remains important for discovery, art direction, brand fit, client approvals, and quality control.
narrative.value_capture: Savings are vulnerable to renewal repricing because work is often sold by project or hour and customers can compare alternatives. Retainers, brand-system knowledge, and fixed scopes offer better retention, but no public 541430 evidence directly measures post-AI margin retention.
narrative.firm_availability: Census reports 15,828 employer establishments in 541430, demonstrating a sizable employer base, but that figure does not identify the LMM, recurring-service, or transferable subset. Broad owner-aging evidence supports succession potential while leaving actual qualifying transfers uncertain.
narrative.demand_durability: Digital-presence needs support ongoing visual-communication demand, yet BLS projects only 2 percent Graphic Designer employment growth across 2024 to 2034 and warns that AI may reduce freelance contracting. Lower-complexity design can migrate to self-service tools.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The key uncertainties are revenue recurrence, owner dependence, customer concentration, client willingness to pay for faster production, and the pace at which self-service tools displace agency work. AI-generated material also brings disclosure and human-authorship considerations in copyright registration, increasing review needs for some deliverables.
narrative.principal_driver: A substantial portion of design production can be accelerated while recurring agency relationships may preserve demand for accountable creative and production oversight.
narrative.principal_weakness: The industry is fragmented and project-oriented, while AI-enabled self-service and fee repricing can erode both demand and retained savings.

sources.title: Census NAICS description for 541430 Graphic Design Services
sources.supports: Defines the industry's visual-communication activities and its included service categories.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Graphic Designers
sources.supports: Lists representative Graphic Designer tasks including graphics and layouts, concepts and sample layouts, computer-generated graphics, and collaboration.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Graphic Designers
sources.supports: Reports 2 percent projected Graphic Designer employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and notes that AI may reduce contracting with freelance graphic designers.

sources.title: Census Bureau Profile: 541430 Graphic Design Services
sources.supports: Reports 15,828 total employer establishments for 541430 in 2023 County Business Patterns.

sources.title: SNAAP: Adoption, Applications, and Attitudes Toward AI Among Arts and Design Alumni
sources.supports: Reports that just over half of surveyed arts and design alumni use generative AI in their work and that adoption is uneven.

sources.title: U.S. Copyright Office: Copyright Registration Guidance for Works Containing AI-Generated Material
sources.supports: Explains registration guidance for works containing AI-generated material and the need to disclose appreciable AI-generated content.

sources.title: Associated Press: Starting a small business is hard. Exiting can be even harder
sources.supports: Reports that 51 percent of small-business owners are over age 55, citing U.S. Census data, and discusses sale, succession, and closure paths.
